response:
You can definitely solve this problem without needing to set an environment variable every time.
The Flask CLI automatically detects the context in which your application is being invoked.
When you run `flask my_command`, the system knows it is executing a CLI task.
This means you do not need to rely on external variables like `FLASK_MY_VARIABLE` for detection.
You can simply check the current context inside your `create_app` function to determine the mode.
The `flask` command line tool passes specific signals that you can inspect directly.
One effective approach is to check if the `app` object is being created for a CLI context.
You might notice that the `flask run` command behaves differently than `flask my_command`.
This distinction allows you to branch your logic based on how the app was started.
You can use the `click` context object to see if a command is currently being executed.
If a command is active, you can set a flag inside your configuration to indicate CLI mode.
This flag can then be used to load a different configuration or create a specific app instance.
Another option is to define a custom `create_app` function specifically for CLI usage.
You can name this function `create_cli_app` and register it with the CLI loader.
The Flask CLI supports multiple factory functions if you configure the `FLASK_APP` environment correctly.
However, you usually want a single entry point that adapts to the situation automatically.
By inspecting the `sys.argv` list, you can see exactly which command was requested by the user.
If the second argument is `run`, you know it is a server request, otherwise it is a CLI command.
This logic can be embedded directly in your main application file without any extra setup.
You will find that this method is much cleaner than managing multiple environment variables manually.
